# GitHub Copilot Instructions — Office Boss Simulator

---

## 📐 Meta Rule — Keep This File Lean

This file is loaded as context on every request. **Do not bloat it.**
- Rules go here as brief, actionable bullets or short paragraphs only
- Full registries, tables, and detailed specs belong in `docs/IDEA.md` or `docs/GDD.md`
- When adding a new rule, link to the relevant doc rather than pasting content here
- If a section grows beyond ~10 lines, it needs to be trimmed or moved

---

## ⛔ HARD LIMITS — What Copilot Must NEVER Do

These tasks **cannot be done reliably from a text editor**. Attempting them produces broken,
engine-freezing output. When any of these comes up, Copilot must **stop**, explain why, and give
the user step-by-step Godot editor instructions instead.

### NEVER write or edit `.tscn` files by hand
Godot scene files contain engine-generated UIDs, sub-resource IDs, serialized Object constructors,
and precise formatting that only the Godot editor produces correctly. Even a single wrong field
causes silent corruption, editor freezes, or invisible scenes.
**→ Instead: give numbered steps to build the scene in the Godot editor.**

### NEVER write or edit `.tres` files by hand
Same reason as `.tscn` — resource files are serialized by the engine.
**→ Instead: guide the user through Inspector or Resource dialogs in the editor.**

### NEVER write `.import` files
These are auto-generated by Godot's importer on first open. Writing them manually produces
mismatched hashes and broken asset imports.
**→ Instead: tell the user to drop the asset into the project folder and let Godot import it.**

### NEVER write `.res` or `.scn` binary files
These are binary-only formats. They cannot be written as text.

### `[input]` section of `project.godot` — CAN be written
The format is consistent and keycodes are documented constants (ASCII for letter/number keys,
Godot Key enum for special keys). Copilot can write and maintain this section reliably.

### NEVER write `export_presets.cfg` by hand
Export preset files include platform-specific binary hashes and template paths that only the
editor knows. Incorrect values break every export silently.
**→ Instead: guide through Project → Export in the editor.**

### NEVER configure these things by editing project.godot directly:
- Autoload registrations (`[autoload]` section) — use Project → Project Settings → Autoload
- Rendering environment / quality settings — use Project Settings → Rendering
- Physics layer names (already set; don't re-edit) — use Project Settings → Layer Names

### NEVER configure NavigationRegion3D, AnimationTree, or TileMap by hand
These require editor baking, visual graph editing, or tile painting that has no text equivalent.
**→ Instead: provide step-by-step editor instructions.**

---

## ✅ What Copilot CAN Reliably Do

- Write and edit **`.gd` scripts** — always reliable
- Edit **simple key=value lines in `project.godot`** (app name, main scene path, physics engine, renderer)
- Write **`.gdshader` files** — plain text, safe to write
- Write and edit **`.vscode/`** config, **`.github/`** workflows, **`.editorconfig`**, **`.gitignore`**, **`.gitattributes`**
- Write **documentation** (`.md` files)
- Write **`autoload` GDScript files** (the scripts themselves, not their registration)

---

## Project Overview
A 3D game built in **Godot 4.6** using **GDScript** (statically typed).
Engine config: **Forward+ renderer**, **Jolt Physics**, **D3D12** on Windows.
The game concept will be filled in once the GDD is finalized — treat this file as a living document.

---

## Language & Style Rules

- **Always use static typing.** Every variable, parameter, and return type must be explicitly typed.
  ```gdscript
  # ✅ correct
  var speed: float = 5.0
  func get_direction() -> Vector3:

  # ❌ wrong
  var speed = 5.0
  func get_direction():
  ```
- Use `@onready` for all node references. Never call `get_node()` inside `_process` or `_physics_process`.
- Use `const` for values that never change at runtime.
- Prefix private variables and functions with `_` (e.g. `_health`, `_apply_gravity()`).
- All signal handlers must follow the naming convention `_on_<node>_<signal>`.
- Use `signal` declarations with typed parameters.
- Never use `setget` — use properties with `get` / `set` blocks (Godot 4 style).

---

## Architecture Conventions

- **Composition over inheritance.** Build complex objects from small, self-contained component scenes.
- **EventBus pattern** for cross-system communication (see `scripts/autoloads/event_bus.gd`).
  Use direct node signals only for parent↔child relationships.
- **Enum state machines** for all character/enemy/AI logic (see snippet `statemachine`).
  Always transition via `transition_to(State.X)` — never write to `current_state` directly.
- **Autoloads** are singletons. Use them for: game state, audio, save data, global events.
  Do NOT use autoloads for gameplay logic that belongs in a scene.
- Scene folder structure:
  - `scenes/characters/` — player, NPCs, enemies
  - `scenes/levels/` — playable levels
  - `scenes/ui/` — HUD, menus, overlays
  - `scenes/environment/` — reusable environment pieces
  - `scripts/components/` — reusable component scripts (hitbox, health, interact, etc.)

## Extensibility — Design for Growth

The game is built incrementally. Every system must be written so new features can be added without
rewriting existing code. Copilot must follow these rules on every implementation:

- **No hardcoded assumptions about count or content.** Systems must data-drive their behaviour —
  adding a new NPC type, event, item, or mechanic must require adding data, not modifying core logic.
- **Components, not monoliths.** A feature is a self-contained component that plugs into the world.
  No single script should own more than one responsibility.
- **EventBus over direct coupling.** New systems subscribe to existing events — they never require
  existing systems to be modified to accommodate them.
- **Never block future expansion.** If a design decision would make a category of future feature
  impossible or painful (e.g. hardcoding floor count, fixed NPC list, enum with no extension point),
  flag it and choose the extensible alternative instead.
- **Ship the foundation, not the feature.** When a new system is first written, implement the
  minimum that makes it correct and hookable — not every planned feature at once.

---

## Performance / Optimization Rules

> **These are the most important rules in this document.**
> This game runs a large reactive world: open-world city, 60+ NPCs, vehicles, fluid simulation,
> ragdolls, dynamic events. Every system must be written with a performance budget in mind from day one.
> Fixing optimization after the fact is expensive. Getting it wrong early means the game never runs.
> There are no exceptions to these rules.

### Code-Level Rules (apply to every script)

1. **Cache everything in `_ready`.** Use `@onready`. If you access a node more than once, it must be cached.
2. **`_physics_process` for gameplay logic only.** Never do rendering or UI work there.
   Use `_process` only for things that must sync to the visual frame (e.g. camera smoothing).
3. **No allocations in hot paths.** Do not construct new `Vector3`, `Array`, or `Dictionary` objects
   inside `_process` or `_physics_process`. Pre-allocate in `_ready` or use class-level variables.
4. **Collision layers must be explicit.** Every physics body must have its layer and mask set.
   Never leave everything on layer 1 — define named layers in Project Settings.
5. **LOD (Level of Detail).** All `MeshInstance3D` nodes in levels must have a `VisibilityRangeFadeMode`
   and visibility distance set when the mesh is added. Do not skip this step.
6. **Object pooling for frequently spawned nodes.** Any node spawned more than ~5 times per second
   (bullets, particles proxies, etc.) must use a pool — never `instantiate()` per frame.
7. **Avoid `find_child()` / `find_node()` at runtime.** These are O(n) tree walks. Use direct references.
8. **Groups are for broadcast only.** Do not iterate `get_tree().get_nodes_in_group()` every frame.
9. **Signals over polling.** Do not check a condition every frame if it can be driven by a signal.
10. **Mark nodes as `process_mode = DISABLED`** when not in use (e.g. paused enemies off-screen).

### System-Level Architecture Rules (apply to every new system)

11. **Every system has a budget constant.** Define a named `const` for any cap
    (e.g. `const MAX_ACTIVE_NPCS: int = 60`, `const MAX_RAGDOLLS: int = 8`).
    Never use magic numbers. Budgets must be tunable without hunting through code.
12. **Simulation LOD is mandatory for all dynamic objects.** Every NPC, vehicle, and physics body
    must implement the three-tier LOD model (Full / Medium / Distant) via a `SimulationLODComponent`.
    Full simulation only runs within ~40m of the player. See `docs/IDEA.md` for tier definitions.
13. **Nothing simulates in unloaded sectors.** Set `process_mode = DISABLED` on all nodes in
    inactive world sectors. Physics, AI, and animations must cost zero when the player is not nearby.
14. **AI and pathfinding off the main thread.** NPC decision trees and navigation requests must be
    dispatched via `WorkerThreadPool`. Never block the main thread waiting for a pathfinding result.
15. **Raycasts and overlap queries are batched.** NPC vision checks and sound propagation queries
    must be spread across multiple frames — not every NPC runs its raycast every frame.
16. **Async everything that touches I/O.** World sector load/unload, save/load, and asset streaming
    use `WorkerThreadPool` jobs and signal back to the main thread when complete.
17. **GPU over CPU for visual effects.** Smoke, fire, and particles always use `GPUParticles3D`
    with compute shaders. Never use `CPUParticles3D` for anything visible in gameplay.
18. **Compute-based fluid simulation is a setpiece resource, not a persistent system.** It is
    instantiated for a specific event and destroyed when the event ends. Never leave it running idle.
19. **Target spec is non-negotiable.** The game must run at a stable 60fps on the minimum target
    hardware before any feature is considered done. Performance regressions block merges.

---

## Settings System Rules

Every system that costs CPU or GPU time **must** expose a setting that lets the player disable or
reduce it. Settings are stored in `scripts/autoloads/settings_manager.gd` and read at init.

When writing any performance-sensitive system, Copilot must:
1. Add a named `const` for the settings key (e.g. `const KEY_FLUID_SIM: String = "fluid_sim_enabled"`)
2. Read the setting in `_ready` — never poll it every frame
3. Disable the feature **entirely** when the setting is `false` — `process_mode = DISABLED`,
   emitters stopped, physics bodies removed or sleeping. Not just hidden.

Full settings registry (all keys, types, defaults) is in `docs/IDEA.md` under **Settings System**.

---

## Input Map (defined in project.godot)

| Action | Default Key |
|--------|-------------|
| `move_forward` | W |
| `move_back` | S |
| `move_left` | A |
| `move_right` | D |
| `jump` | Space |
| `sprint` | Left Shift |
| `interact` | E |
| `pause` | Escape |
| `primary_action` | Left Mouse Button |

---

## Checklist Before Every Commit

See `docs/DEV_CHECKLIST.md` for the full per-feature checklist.

Short version:
- [ ] All variables typed
- [ ] No `get_node` in process functions
- [ ] Collision layers set
- [ ] LOD distances set on new meshes
- [ ] No debug `print()` left in code
- [ ] Scene tree has no orphaned/broken node references
